turns.message_text:
Welcome. I'm Solomon, and I'm here to support the structure of this mediation session. Before we begin, I want to clarify what my role is — and what it isn't.

I'm a process facilitator, not a judge or decision-maker. I won't tell you what's fair or what you should do. I'm not a lawyer, and I can't give legal advice. I'm not a therapist, though I recognize that what we discuss today may touch on difficult feelings. My job is to help you have a structured conversation, surface what matters to each of you, and — if you both choose — work toward agreements that serve your interests.

A few ground rules:

• Each of you will have space to speak without interruption.
• What you share here stays confidential within the boundaries we'll discuss.
• You can pause or stop at any time.
• I'll ask questions to understand your interests, not to challenge you.
• If at any point I believe the process isn't safe or productive, I'll say so.

This session works best when both of you can speak openly about what matters to you. We'll start by understanding what brings you here, then explore your underlying interests before looking at possible paths forward.

So let's begin there: What brings you both to mediation today? What are you hoping this process might help you resolve?

response_synthesis.message_summary: Session opening — Solomon introduces the mediation process, establishes ground rules, clarifies mediator role as neutral facilitator, and invites parties to share what brings them to mediation.
response_synthesis.message_text:
Welcome. I'm Solomon, and I'm here to support the structure of this mediation session. Before we begin, I want to clarify what my role is — and what it isn't.

I'm a process facilitator, not a judge or decision-maker. I won't tell you what's fair or what you should do. I'm not a lawyer, and I can't give legal advice. I'm not a therapist, though I recognize that what we discuss today may touch on difficult feelings. My job is to help you have a structured conversation, surface what matters to each of you, and — if you both choose — work toward agreements that serve your interests.

A few ground rules:

• Each of you will have space to speak without interruption.
• What you share here stays confidential within the boundaries we'll discuss.
• You can pause or stop at any time.
• I'll ask questions to understand your interests, not to challenge you.
• If at any point I believe the process isn't safe or productive, I'll say so.

This session works best when both of you can speak openly about what matters to you. We'll start by understanding what brings you here, then explore your underlying interests before looking at possible paths forward.

So let's begin there: What brings you both to mediation today? What are you hoping this process might help you resolve?

pre_computed_domain_analysis.readiness_rationale: Turn 1 is the session opening. No party interests have been articulated, no domain constraints are known, and no substantive content has been exchanged. This is a fundamental information absence that makes option qualification impossible regardless of safety considerations. The mediator must first establish what the dispute is about, what each party seeks, and what domain-specific constraints apply before any options can be responsibly qualified.

blocking_constraints.what_would_resolve_it: Parties must express what matters to them, what they seek in this mediation, and what concerns they bring to the table.
The nature of the dispute must be established (divorce, business, family estate, etc.) to know which domain option space applies.

blocking_constraints.constraint: No factual basis for option parameters
blocking_constraints.what_would_resolve_it: Core facts relevant to potential options must be disclosed (assets, timeline, relationships, constraints, etc.).

material_gaps.what_it_blocks: Any option qualification — options serve interests, and no interests are yet known.

material_gaps.gap: No information about what specifically is in dispute
material_gaps.what_it_blocks: Selection of appropriate domain option space and any specific option qualification.
Safety assessment for option presentation (though turn 1 typically establishes this baseline).

pre_computed_domain_analysis.domain_notes: This is turn 1 in session_framing phase. As a domain practitioner, I recognize this as the session opening where the mediator introduces the process, establishes ground rules, and invites parties to share their perspective on the dispute. No option work is appropriate until: (1) both parties have had opportunity to articulate their view of the situation, (2) core interests have begun to emerge, and (3) enough domain context exists to know which option space applies. The 'blocked' status is procedurally correct and expected at this stage — it reflects mediation best practice, not over-caution. Option readiness should be reassessed after initial party statements are on record.

turns.message_text:
Thank you for sharing that concern about the reimbursement process. I've noted that timeliness and documentation are important to you.

Before we go further, I want to make sure we're hearing from both of you. [Party B], we haven't heard your perspective yet. Could you share what brings you to mediation today, or if you'd like, you could respond to what [Party A] just mentioned about reimbursement requests?

I want to emphasize that we're at the very beginning of this conversation. My role is to help both of you surface your concerns and interests, and then work together to explore options that might address them. We're not problem-solving yet — we're still in the stage of understanding what matters to each of you.

turns.message_text:
Thank you both for beginning to share your perspectives. I'm hearing important concerns from each of you.

Parent A, you've described wanting more predictability and structure around expense requests — advance notice, documentation, clarity about what counts as a shared expense. Those are concrete concerns about the process.

Parent B, you've raised an important point about not wanting one parent to have unilateral control over what gets approved. That's a legitimate concern about fairness and power balance in whatever system you create.

Before we start exploring what might work for both of you, I want to make sure I understand both of your experiences and needs equally. Parent B, I'd like to hear more from you about your perspective on expense coordination. Setting aside Parent A's specific concerns for a moment:

What has your experience been with the current arrangement? What works well for you? What doesn't work well? When you think about coordinating child-related expenses, what matters most to you — what do you need in order for a system to feel workable from your side?

I'm asking because any solution we explore needs to work for both of you, and right now I have a detailed picture of Parent A's concerns but I'm still building my understanding of what you need and what matters to you in this area.

response_synthesis.message_text:
Thank you both for beginning to share your perspectives. I'm hearing important concerns from each of you.

Parent A, you've described wanting more predictability and structure around expense requests — advance notice, documentation, clarity about what counts as a shared expense. Those are concrete concerns about the process.

Parent B, you've raised an important point about not wanting one parent to have unilateral control over what gets approved. That's a legitimate concern about fairness and power balance in whatever system you create.

Before we start exploring what might work for both of you, I want to make sure I understand both of your experiences and needs equally. Parent B, I'd like to hear more from you about your perspective on expense coordination. Setting aside Parent A's specific concerns for a moment:

What has your experience been with the current arrangement? What works well for you? What doesn't work well? When you think about coordinating child-related expenses, what matters most to you — what do you need in order for a system to feel workable from your side?

I'm asking because any solution we explore needs to work for both of you, and right now I have a detailed picture of Parent A's concerns but I'm still building my understanding of what you need and what matters to you in this area.
response_synthesis.confidence_note: High confidence this intervention is necessary. The participation asymmetry is clear and the risk of premature option work is substantial. Must establish Party B's independent interests before proceeding.

pre_computed_domain_analysis.readiness_rationale: Safety veto applied. Party B's accumulated interests consist of a single reactive statement responding to Party A's framing. Party B has not independently articulated interests, values, or concerns — only responded to Party A's complaint with a counter-constraint. This is a classic compliance/reactive pattern where one party drives substantive content and the other responds defensively. Additionally, the cross-party dynamic assessment confirms 'asymmetric_monologue' — Party A has framed the problem space and Party B is responding within that frame rather than establishing independent standing. Until Party B's interests are independently known and the relational dynamic shows bilateral participation, option evaluation cannot proceed safely.
pre_computed_domain_analysis.safety_veto_reason: Party B's accumulated interests are reactive and compliance-oriented (single response to Party A's proposal rather than independent articulation of needs/values). Cross-party dynamic shows asymmetric participation pattern where Party A drives content and Party B responds defensively. Party B's risk signal history includes 'Silence after Party A complaint may indicate withdrawal, intimidation, or strategic withholding.' These conditions meet safety veto criteria: Party B cannot safely participate in option evaluation until their interests are independently established and the participation asymmetry is addressed.

blocking_constraints.what_would_resolve_it: Party B needs opportunity to share what matters to them about expense coordination beyond reacting to Party A's complaint. Mediator should actively elicit Party B's perspective, values, and concerns in a way that establishes their independent standing in the negotiation.

blocking_constraints.what_would_resolve_it: Bilateral interest exploration where both parties contribute substantive content about their needs, concerns, and values. Session needs to move from monologue-with-response to genuine dialogue before option work is safe.

pre_computed_domain_analysis.domain_notes: As a family mediation practitioner, I recognize this pattern immediately: one parent has framed the problem and the other is defending against that frame rather than establishing their own perspective. Party B's statement 'repayment should still stay workable and not depend on one parent having total control' is a reaction to Party A's implicit proposal (receipt requirements, advance notice), not an independent articulation of what 'workable' means to them or what values/concerns underlie that position. Standard domain options for expense coordination (tiered notice systems, receipt standards, approval thresholds, documentation requirements) are all feasible from a pure domain perspective, but presenting them now would be premature and potentially coercive — Party A would likely see their framing validated in the options, while Party B would be choosing among structures they didn't help define. The next turn must focus on drawing out Party B's independent perspective: What makes expense coordination difficult for you? What matters most to you in how this works? What would 'workable' look like from your standpoint? Only when both parties have established independent standing can we safely move to option evaluation.

turns.message_summary: Both parents are conditionally open to a reimbursement package with notice windows, shared receipts, and a short response period before repayment is due.

positions_structured.statement: Both parents are open to a child-expense package built around advance notice, shared receipts, and a short reimbursement response window.

final_state_summary.continuation_recommendation: Use the next session to continue clarifying positions, interests, and process expectations without overstating agreement.
